turns.content: I'm back in the loop after five years, and I'm looking at this new codebase. I've read about chumsky and recursive descent parsing before, but when I tried to implement something similar without error recovery, my parser just died on every malformed input. It feels like I understand the theory but can't quite grasp how to handle real-world messy input.

Welcome back! That exact frustration is common when moving from a toy parser to production code. The core idea with chumsky is that you define your grammar as a series of rules using traits like Parser and Chomsky, but the real magic happens in how you chain those parsers together. Instead of just returning an Option on failure, you use combinators like alt or rest to try multiple alternatives before giving up.

I see where you're going with that, but I think you might be oversimplifying it. When I tried chaining parsers in a previous project, the moment there was an unexpected token or a missing semicolon, the whole parse tree just collapsed and I got no AST at all. It seems like without proper error recovery strategies, chumsky is basically useless for anything beyond simple expressions.

You're absolutely right to push back there; that's the main difference between academic parsers and practical ones. In a real implementation with chumsky, you don't just let one rule fail and stop; you use recovery techniques like goto after error or skip until next token. For example, if your parser expects a `;` but finds something else instead, you can define a recovery rule that consumes tokens until it hits the `;` again, allowing the parse to continue building the AST even with errors.
Okay, so I get the idea of skipping tokens, but how do you actually implement this in chumsky? Is there a built-in recovery combinator or do I have to write custom logic for every error case?

chumsky doesn't provide a single magic recovery combinator, so you typically compose your own strategies using the standard combinators. For instance, if you define a rule that expects an identifier followed by `:`, and gets something else instead, you can use the `rest` combinator to consume tokens until you find the expected token again, then re-apply your original rule. This way, your parser recovers gracefully rather than crashing.

That makes sense conceptually, but I'm still worried about how this affects the AST structure. If we're recovering from errors by skipping tokens, won't that mess up the span information for the nodes in my tree?

Great observation; spans are critical for debugging and source highlighting. With chumsky, you can track token positions using a Span type or similar structure alongside your parse results. When you implement recovery, you need to carefully manage where the span starts and ends so that even if tokens are skipped during error handling, the final nodes in your AST still have accurate spans reflecting what was actually parsed successfully.

I'm starting to see how important that is. But I imagine there's a lot of boilerplate involved in tracking spans correctly with all these recovery strategies. Is there any way to simplify this without sacrificing correctness?

You can definitely reduce the boilerplate by designing your grammar rules so that error conditions are predictable and common. For example, if most errors happen at specific boundaries like between statements or after expressions, you can create generic recovery patterns that apply to those cases rather than writing a unique recovery rule for every possible error. This keeps your code clean while still handling real-world input robustly.

That sounds promising, but I'm still not sure how this compares to traditional recursive descent parsers without chumsky. Do you think the explicit recovery strategies make up for any complexity added by using a library like this?

The trade-off is interesting; traditional recursive descent often requires manual backtracking or lookahead tables to implement error recovery, which can be tedious and error-prone. With chumsky, the declarative nature of defining your grammar rules combined with explicit combinators for recovery makes it easier to reason about and modify. You still write the logic, but you're working with a more structured approach that reduces the chance of subtle bugs in your parser.

I think I'm getting closer to understanding this now. One last question: when building an AST for something like a simple language, how do you ensure that the nodes reflect only the successfully parsed parts and not include garbage from the recovery phase?

You construct your AST by collecting results only from rules that fully succeeded. During recovery phases, you might accumulate tokens in a separate buffer or simply ignore them entirely so they never get added to the final tree structure. Once all parsing is complete and any necessary recoveries are done, you build your data structures using only the successful parse results, ensuring your AST remains clean and accurate despite encountering errors during processing.
